texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 separation technology has been applied widely in various fields, such as CO2  capture from flue gas, purification of natural gas, syngas, and biogas [1 -6]. CO2 separation technology plays an important role in reducing greenhouse gas emissions and improving the utilization efficiency  of  energy  gas.  Membrane  technology  has  attracted  more  and more attention in the field of CO2  separation because of its environmental friendliness, simple operation, and low energy consumption [7]. The development of CO2 separation membrane with high gas permeance and CO2 selectivity has always been the focus of researches. Multi-layer composite membrane is a good choice for the preparation of membrane with high permselectivity due to its advantages of simple preparation and easy scaling up process [8]. For example, the reported large-area CO2 separation membranes of PolyActive ™ prepared by Helmholtz-Zentrum Geesthacht and Polaris ™ prepared by Membrane Technology  and  Research,  Inc.  (MTR)  are  all  in  the  form  of  the multi-layer  composite  membrane  [9,10].  The  multi-layer  composite membrane usually consists of a support layer, intermediate layer (or gutter layer), and separation layer. Polysulfone (PS), polyethersulfone

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Preparation of CO2 separation membrane with high gas permeance and CO2 selectivity is always desirable. In this work, inspired by pesticides and cosmetics, through the use of a variety of additives, good compatibility between the water-based coating solution of low concentration and the hydrophobic surface of the intermediate layer (or gutter  layer)  had  been  achieved.  A  membrane  with  high  gas  permeance  and  moderate  CO2  selectivity  was prepared preliminarily, and the synergistic mechanism among various additives was clarified. Then, through simple cross-linking modification of the membrane surface, a multi-layer composite membrane for CO2  separation was prepared with CO2 selectivity being improved greatly. The degree of cross-linking on the membrane surface and the thickness of the cross-linked layer was studied by XPS etching analysis. Separation performance for CO2/N2, CO2/CH4, and CO2/H2 mixture had been tested, and the prepared multi-layer composite membrane exhibited excellent CO2 separation performance. Moreover, the simulated flue gas test showed that the prepared membrane was of good long-term stability and impurity resistance.

texts.orig: (PES) and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) ultrafiltration membranes are usually chosen  as  support  layers  [11 -13].  The  development  of  intermediate layer and separation layer is the focus in current research. The main function of the intermediate layer is to prevent pore penetration of the separation layer, and the gas transport resistance in intermediate layer must be small enough. Using traditional polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), Chung et al. prepared an intermediate layer with extremely high gas permeance and systematically investigated the effects of various fabrication parameters, including the pre-wetting agents and coating solution viscosity [14,15]. Park et al. prepared a new-type Teflon AF2400 intermediate layer of 75 nm thickness, showing CO2 permeance of 31500 and CO2/N2 selectivity of 3.36 [16]. For the separation layer, to achieve high gas permeance, it is usually needed to ensure that the membrane material has a large free volume or can be swelled to a high degree in the separation  process,  and  the  layer  thickness  is  thin  enough.  As  for selectivity, it should be improved mainly from three aspects: solution, diffusivity, and reaction. In order to prepare a high-performance separation layer in the multi-layer composite membrane, the problems as follows must be solved: how to prepare membrane material with high permeability and selectivity, how to fabricate the membrane material

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the problem of preparing membrane materials with high CO2 permeability and selectivity, there are mainly three kinds of methods to solve  it.  Firstly,  it  can  be  started  from  the  design  of  the  molecular structure  and  functional  groups  to  synthesize  a  brand-new  material, which is the most creative and difficult way. Secondly, modifying the existing  materials  with  high  CO2 selectivity  to  improve  their  CO2 permeability, while ensuring that the CO2 selectivity is still high enough. For example, to improve the permeability of traditional polymer materials, many researchers have chosen to fill dense polymer membranes with  porous  nanomaterials  to  prepare  mixed  matrix  membranes, creating fast CO2  transport pathways in the membrane [17]. Thirdly, modifying  existing  membrane  materials  of  high  CO2  permeability  to improve  their  CO2  selectivity,  while  ensuring  that  their  CO2  permeability  is  still  high  enough.  For  example,  through  heat  treatment  or chemical cross-linking, some researchers successfully improved the CO2 selectivity of some polymer materials with ultra-high gas permeability, such  as  polymers  of  intrinsic  microporosity  (PIMs),  Thermally  rearranged (TR) polymer, poly(trimethyisilylpropyne) (PTMSP) and PDMS [18 -21]. Compared to the fist method, the last two are more common in reported works. In general, cross-linking could inhibit the mobility of polymer chains and improve the diffusivity selectivity of the membrane. Besides, cross-linking could also improve the plasticization resistance, solvent resistance, and long-term stability of the membrane, but often reduce the permeability of the membrane [20,22,23]. There are two main ways for membrane cross-linking, i.e., overall cross-linking and surface cross-linking. The overall cross-linking of the membrane may lead to a significant reduction of the permeability, and the operation is relatively complicated. As for surface cross-linking, the thickness of the cross-linked layer could be smaller, which can reduce the influence on the permeability of the separation layer. In addition, it can be carried out after membrane formation, making the operation more convenient.

texts.orig: As for the preparation of a thin and defect-free separation layer, it is mainly a problem of membrane fabrication technology. At present, the commonly used fabrication methods include coating, interfacial polymerization,  layer-by-layer  self-assembly,  Langmuir  trough,  chemical vapor deposition, and so on [24 -28]. Compared to other methods, the coating is used more widely because of its simple operation and easy scaling-up process. For coating, the methods to reduce the thickness of the separating layer mainly include reducing the thickness of the wet coating layer and reducing the concentration of the coating solution. Because coating a thinner wet coating layer always requires higher accuracy of coating equipment and higher uniformity of supporting layer thickness, reducing the concentration of coating solution is relatively easier to achieve a thin separation layer with better repeatability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ensuring good compatibility between the separation layer and the intermediate  layer  is  crucial  and  difficult  for  preparing  multilayer composite membranes with high performance for CO2 separation. As we know, many materials with high permeability and selectivity for CO2 are hydrophilic  or  alcoholic,  such  as  amine-containing  polymers  (polyvinylamine, polyallylamine, and so on) and polymers containing ether groups (Pebax, PEO, etc.). However, intermediate materials are usually hydrophobic, such as PTMSP and PDMS. For this problem, some researchers had given some good solutions. Ho et al. deposited hydrophilic zeolite Y on the surface of the supporting layer as the intermediate layer to achieve good compatibility with the separation layer of the aminecontaining  polymer  [29].  Through  hydrothermal  growth,  Qiao  et  al. coated the anodic substrate with a hydrophilic MOF layer (NH2-MIL-53) as the intermediate layer and then formed an ultrathin separation layer of PEG via CAP technology [30]. To enhance the hydrophilicity of the intermediate layer, Saito et al. prepared poly(dimethylsiloxane) poly (ethylene glycol) methyl ether acrylate  (PDMS PEGMEA)-based copolymer via thiol ene reaction [31]. Inspired by the mussel, Li et al. deposited  a  thin  layer  of  polydopamine  (PDA)  on  the  surface  of  the PDMS intermediate  layer  achieving  good  compatibility  with  the  hydrophilic separation layers such as polyvinylamine (PVAm) and Pebax [32]. Researchers usually enhanced the compatibility between the intermediate layer and hydrophilic separation layer by replacing the hydrophobic  intermediate  layer  with  a  hydrophilic  one  or  through hydrophilic modification for the traditional hydrophobic intermediate layer.  There  are  few  reports  to  solve  this  problem  by  modifying  the separation layer, because it may cause great damage to its separation performance.  However,  if  we  can  modify  the  hydrophilic  separation layer to achieve good compatibility with the traditional hydrophobic intermediate layer while maintaining the performance of the separation layer, it will be of great benefit to reduce the complexity of the membrane preparation process and fabrication cost. As is known to us all, many pesticides and cosmetics are aqueous solutions or dispersions. In order to play their role better, they need to be well spread on the surface of plant leaves (or seeds) and human skin, respectively, and these surfaces  are  usually  hydrophobic.  Obviously,  we  would  not  modify  the surface of plant leaves or human skin. Maybe we can find some inspiration from pesticides and cosmetics.

texts.orig: N-Vinylformamide (NVF, 98%, stabilized with BHT) and 2, 2 0 -Azobis (2-methylpropionamidine) dihydrochloride (AIBA) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich  (USA).  Polyvinyl  alcohol  (PVA,  99%  hydrolyzed)  and sodium  dodecyl  sulfate  (SDS)  were  provided  by  Shanghai  Aladdin Biochemistry Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium polyacrylate(PAAS, the  molecular  weight  of  5,000,000 Da)was  from  Shanghai  Macklin Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Concentrated hydrochloric acid (36 wt%), dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTD), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), n-heptane, and  deionized  water  were  bought  from  Tianjin  Jiangtian  Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). PDMS prepolymer was from Shin-Etsu Chemical Co. (Japan). Trimesoyl chloride (TMC) was provided by Beijing  J & K  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.  (China).  Polysulfone  ultrafiltration membranes were bought from Shandong Jozzon Membrane Technology Co., Ltd. (China). The chemical structure of PVAm (the key component of the coating solution) and various additives were shown in Fig. 1. It could be seen that except for small molecule of SDS, the other components were all polymers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The preparation of films is mainly for characterization. The coating solutions  containing  SDS,  PVA,  PAAS,  and  PVAm  in  different  proportions  were coated on the glass plate.  After  drying, a  certain concentration of TMC in n-heptane was coated on the surfaces of some films with the unreacted TMC being removed by washing the film surfaces with n-heptane for several times. Then these films were removed from the glass plate.

texts.text: 2.3.2. Preparation of multi-layer composite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Firstly, the PDMS intermediate layer was formed on the surface of a porous  substrate  by  a  wet  coating  method.  PDMS  prepolymer  was completely  dissolved  in  n-heptane  to  form  a  homogeneous  solution. Then the TEOS cross-linking agent and DBTD catalyst were added. The mass ratio of PDMS prepolymer to TEOS and DBTD was 5:4:4. The mixed solution reacted at 35 � C to form a partially cross-linked PDMS solution, which was then stored in an ice-water bath to slow down its further reaction.  PSf  ultrafiltration  membrane  was  chosen  as  the  supporting layer with the surface water being removed, while the water in the pores was retained to inhibit the pore penetration of the PDMS layer. Partially cross-linked PDMS solution was coated onto the surface of PSf ultrafiltration membrane with a home-made coating machine, then it was dried in an artificial climate box for 30 min to form the PDMS/PSf membrane.

TMC was dissolved into n-heptane to form homogeneous solutions in certain concentrations and then were coated onto the surfaces of PPPS/PDMS/PSf membranes. texts.orig: The gas separation performance of the multi-layer composite membranes was tested on a home-made testing platform. Fig. 2 is the schematic of the testing platform, and the membrane testing conditions were presented in Table 2. The feed gas was humidified to be saturated with water vapor before entering the membrane cell. The temperature of the humidifier was kept 10 � C higher than that of the droplets collector and membrane  cell  to  ensure  the  saturation  of  the  water  vapor  in  the membrane cell.  Residual  gas  vented  to  the  air,  and  He  was  used  as sweeping gas on the permeate side. The flow rate of the permeate gas was  measured  by  an  electronic  soap  membrane  flowmeter,  and  the composition  was  measured  by  gas  chromatography  (Agilent  7890B). With the data of the permeate gas composition, pressure difference, and permeate gas flow rate, the gas permeance could be calculated with the unit of GPU, and the selectivity was the ratio of two gas permeance. texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Fig.  3,  a).  Compared  with  pure  water,  the  contact  angle  between 0.075 wt%  PVAm  aqueous  solution  and  PDMS  surface  decreased  to 110.2 � ,  while  the  viscosity  rose  greatly,  from  1 mPa s  to  30.21 mPa s (Fig. 3, b). The increased viscosity was due to the long molecular chain of PVAm. The decrease of contact angle attributed to the coexistence of hydrophilic  chains  containing  amine  groups  and  hydrophobic  chains containing amide groups in PVAm, which was due to the incomplete hydrolysis in the synthesis process. The amine groups in PVAm were of strong hydrophilicity, and some of the amine groups in water could bind with H þ so that the PVAm molecule was charged positively, and the solution showed alkalinity. The hydrophilic chains of PVAm interacted with water molecules, and the hydrophobic chains interacted with the surface of PDMS, thus reducing the surface tension of the solution and the  contact  angle.  Therefore,  PVAm  could  be  regarded  as  a  cationic surfactant, and in fact, it was often used as a flocculant in the industry to remove negatively charged particles from aqueous solutions. When 20% PVA (based on PVAm) was added to the PVAm solution, the contact angle between the solution and PDMS reduced slightly to 106.5 � (Fig. 3, c).  PVA  is  a  kind  of  polymer  known  for  its  excellent  film-forming property. But due to the large number of hydroxyl groups in PVA, the hydrophilicity of PVA is stronger than that of PVAm, so the addition of PVA did not significantly improve the compatibility between the solution  and  the  surface  of  PDMS.  However,  when  10%  SDS  (based  on PVAm) instead of PVA was added to the PVAm solution, the contact angle decreased greatly to 92.1 � (Fig. 3, d). As we know that SDS is a common anionic surfactant. It consists of a long hydrophobic carbon chain with 12C atoms and a short hydrophilic sulfate end. The molecular structure of SDS and electrostatic interaction between SDS and PVAm was responsible for the improvement of the compatibility between the solution and the PDMS surface, and this interaction can be inferred from the change of the solution viscosity. With the addition of SDS, the viscosity  of  the  solution  changed  from  30.21  mPa  s  to  35.32  mPa  s. Compared with PVAm, the carbon chain of SDS showed stronger hydrophobicity and affinity with the PDMS surface. At the same time, the negative hydrophilic end of SDS and the positive hydrophilic part of PVAm could be connected by electrostatic attraction, which enhanced the interaction between the whole solution system and PDMS surface, thus  greatly  reducing  the  contact  angle.  However,  when  the  coating solution (PVAm þ 20%PVA þ 10%SDS) was used to prepare the composite  membrane,  the  reproducibility  of  its  spreadability  on  the PDMS surface was not good enough. The thickness of the wet coating layer had an influence on the spreadability of the coating solution, and the shrinkage phenomenon would occur when the thickness of the wet coating layer was relatively thin. When 1% PAAS (based on PVAm) was added to the solution of PVAm þ 10%SDS, it was found that the contact angle  decreased  greatly  to  57.3 � (Fig.  3,  h),  which  meant  that  the aqueous solution showed good compatibility with the surface of PDMS. The  above  phenomena  were  caused  by  the  synergetic  effect  among PVAm, PAAS, and SDS, as shown in Fig. 4. As a cationic polymer with strong  hydrophilicity,  PVAm  could  combine  with  a  large  number  of water molecules, while as an anionic polymer, PAAS contains a large number of carboxylate anions which could combine with the cations in PVAm by electrostatic interaction. In fact, some researchers had utilized this electrostatic interaction to do the research of layer-by-layer selfassembly [34,35]. Besides, PAAS has a long molecular chain (the molecular  weight  of  PAAS  used  in  this  work  reaches  5,000,000 Da). Therefore,  PAAS  and  PVAm  could  form  a  cross-linked  hydrophilic network through this electrostatic interaction, which could be inferred from the change of solution viscosity. With the addition of PAAS, the solution viscosity rose greatly from 35.32 mPa s to 63.43 mPa s (Fig. 3, h). At the same time, the anionic hydrophilic ends of SDS could bind to the cationic sites in the hydrophilic network, and the hydrophobic ends bonded to the surface of PDMS so that the whole aqueous solution system could achieve good compatibility with the surface of PDMS.

The  above  phenomena  were  caused  by  the  synergetic  effect  among PVAm, PAAS, and SDS, as shown in Fig. 4. As a cationic polymer with strong  hydrophilicity,  PVAm  could  combine  with  a  large  number  of water molecules, while as an anionic polymer, PAAS contains a large number of carboxylate anions which could combine with the cations in PVAm by electrostatic interaction. In fact, some researchers had utilized this electrostatic interaction to do the research of layer-by-layer selfassembly [34,35]. Besides, PAAS has a long molecular chain (the molecular  weight  of  PAAS  used  in  this  work  reaches  5,000,000 Da). Therefore,  PAAS  and  PVAm  could  form  a  cross-linked  hydrophilic network through this electrostatic interaction, which could be inferred from the change of solution viscosity. With the addition of PAAS, the solution viscosity rose greatly from 35.32 mPa s to 63.43 mPa s (Fig. 3, h). At the same time, the anionic hydrophilic ends of SDS could bind to the cationic sites in the hydrophilic network, and the hydrophobic ends bonded to the surface of PDMS so that the whole aqueous solution system could achieve good compatibility with the surface of PDMS.

texts.orig: Both the permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity showed dependence on the feed pressure. For the strong dependence of the permeance on the feed pressure, it was caused by the carrier approaching saturation [39], membrane compaction [40],  and  membrane  plasticization  [41].  The dependence of the CO2/N2 selectivity on the feed pressure was caused by the difference influence of feed pressure on the CO2 and N2 permeance. Because  of  the  carrier  approaching  saturation,  the  CO2  permeance decreased with the rise of the feed pressure. The N2 permeance slightly decreased when the feed pressure changed from 0.11 MPa to 1.0 MPa, which was due to the membrane compaction. However, when the feed pressure was higher than 1.0 MPa, the N2 permeance increased with the increasing feed pressure, which was caused by the membrane plasticization. The permeation testing results presented in Fig. 5 indicated that the membrane showed high gas permeance but low resistance to plasticization.  At  the  same  time,  under  the  same  feed  pressure,  the  CO2 permeance (Fig. 5, a) and CO2/N2  selectivity (Fig. 5, b) of PPPS membranes were both higher than those of PPS membranes, which was due to the higher water uptake capacity of PPPS layer than that of PPS without PVA. Fig.  6  shows  that  the  water  uptake  capacity  of  PPPS  film  was 63.29% at 25 � C and 90% relative humidity, while that of PPS film was 47.51%. This was due to the molecular structure of the PVA. PVA, which contains a large number of hydroxyl groups, is usually produced by the hydrolysis of polyvinyl acetate and has a strong water uptake ability. It was always used as the main material of hydrogels [42 -44]. The higher the degree of hydrolysis, the more hydroxyl groups in the molecular chain of PVA and the stronger its water uptake ability. The hydrolysis degree of PVA used in this work was as high as 99%. Compared with PPS,  PPPS  had  higher  water  uptake  capacity,  which  led  to  a  higher swelling degree, larger free volume, and thus higher CO2 permeance. At the same time, the presence of more water molecules in the membrane promoted the reversible reaction between the  amine group and  CO2 molecule,  enhanced  the  reaction  selectivity,  and  thus  improved  the CO2/N2  selectivity.  Overall,  PPPS  membranes  exhibited  higher  CO2 separation  performance  with  high  CO2 permeance  and  moderate CO2/N2 selectivity. For example, at 0.2 MPa, its CO2 permeance reached 2193 GPU, and CO2/N2 selectivity was 64.

texts.text: 3.3. Effect of surface cross-linking on membrane performance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  series  of  T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf  multi-layer  composite  membranes were prepared by surface cross-linking of PPPS/PDMS/PSf membranes with different concentrations of TMC. The results of the CO2/N2  separation performance test of the membranes were shown in Fig. 7.
texts.text: A  series  of  T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf  multi-layer  composite  membranes were prepared by surface cross-linking of PPPS/PDMS/PSf membranes with different concentrations of TMC. The results of the CO2/N2  separation performance test of the membranes were shown in Fig. 7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared Fig. 7 (a) with Fig. 5 (a), when TMC was used to cross-link the membrane  surfaces, the  CO2 permeance  of  the  membranes decreased.  Furthermore,  the  permeance  of  CO2 decreased  with  the increasing  TMC  concentration  from  0.05 wt%  to  0.3 wt%.  Besides, compared to the uncross-linked PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane, the permeance  of  N2 in  the  cross-linked  membranes  decreased,  and  the decrease degree was greater than that of CO2, which directly led to the improvement of CO2/N2  selectivity. As we know, the reason why gas molecules can transport in the dense polymeric membrane is that there are inter-chain transient gaps, which is caused by the movement of the polymer  segments.  Therefore,  it  could  be  speculated  that  the  crosslinking  may  limit  the  mobility  of  polymer  segments  in  the  crosslinking  layer,  thus  reducing  the  diffusivity  of  gas  molecules  in  the cross-linked layer, and may have a greater impact on N2 molecules than on CO2.  At the same time, with the consumption of amine groups, the adsorption of CO2  molecules on the membrane surface would also be weakened. As shown in Fig. 7 (b), the variation trend of CO2/N2 selectivity with the addition of TMC concentration was different from that of CO2 permeance. With the increase of TMC concentration from 0.05 wt% to 0.3 wt%, the selectivity of CO2/N2 improved first and then decreased, and the maximum value appeared when TMC concentration was 0.1 wt %.  The  reason  for  this  phenomenon  was  analyzed  as  follows.  The selectivity of the membrane consists of three parts: reaction selectivity (a reversible chemical reaction between amine groups, carboxyl groups, and CO2), physical adsorption selectivity (adsorption of polar groups such as hydroxyl groups to CO2) and diffusivity selectivity. The reaction selectivity of the separation layer includes the chemisorption selectivity on the surface of the membrane, the facilitated transportation selectivity of the cross-linked layer, and the facilitated transportation selectivity of the PPPS layer. Due to the decrease of the carrier concentration, the reaction  selectivity  of  the  membrane  surface  and  cross-linked  layer decreased. For the PPPS, which was not cross-linked by TMC, the carrier concentration  was  not  affected,  and  the  reaction  selectivity  was  not affected. The adsorption of gas molecules on the membrane surface includes  chemical  adsorption  and  physical  adsorption.  The  strength  of physical adsorption depends on the condensation of gas molecules and the intermolecular force between gas molecules and chemical groups on the membrane surface. As CO2 is a quadrupole molecule, polar groups show  greater  physical  adsorption  selectivity  on  CO2/N2 than  the nonpolar groups. In general, the CO2/N2 selectivity of the corresponding polymer can be inferred from the CO2/N2 selectivity in the liquid solvent with the same chemical groups [45]. According to the data reported in the literature [45], at 25 � C, the selectivity of CO2/N2  in methanol, N, N-dimethyl formamide, and methyl acetate is 23, 65, and 36, respectively. In terms of CO2/N2  selectivity, amide group > ester group > hydroxyl group. Some of the amine and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the membrane could react with TMC and converted into amide groups and ester groups. The consumption of amine groups would reduce the chemical adsorption selectivity, and the formation of amide and ester groups  would  enhance  the  physical  adsorption  selectivity.  When  the

texts.orig: concentration of TMC was low ( < 0.1 wt%), the decrease degree of reaction  selectivity  was  less  than  the  increasing  degree  of  diffusivity selectivity, so the selectivity of CO2/N2 would improve with the addition of TMC. However, when the concentration of TMC was higher ( > 0.1 wt %), with the increasing TMC concentration, the decrease degree of reaction  selectivity  exceeded  the  increasing  degree  of  diffusivity  selectivity, resulting in the decrease of CO2/N2 selectivity. Fig. 7 (b) showed that when the feed pressure was lower than 1.0 MPa, the selectivity of the membrane cross-linked with 0.3 wt% TMC was less than that of the PPPS/PDMS/PSf  membrane.  However,  when  the  feed  pressure  was higher than 1.0 MPa, the selectivity of the membrane crosslinked with 0.3 wt% TMC was greater than that of the PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane. It is due to the plasticization of the PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane by CO2 under high feed pressure. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the N2 permeance of the cross-linked  membrane  decreased  slightly  with  the  increasing  feed pressure in the whole testing pressure range, which may be caused by the  compaction effect  [40].  However, for  the  PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane, when the feed pressure was greater than 1.0 MPa, the permeance of N2 rose slightly with the rise of feed pressure. It was indicated that the cross-linking  improved  the  plasticizing  resistance  of  the  membrane. Considering both the CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity, when TMC concentration was 0.1 wt%, the membrane exhibited the best CO2 separation performance. For example, at 0.2 MPa, its CO2  permeance was 1013 GPU, and CO2/N2 selectivity reached 96.

texts.orig: XPS analysis of the N1s in N elements on the membrane surfaces of PPPS and T-PPPS (0.1 wt% TMC) was carried out with the results being shown in Fig. 10. N elements on the surface of the membranes showed two valence states, -NH-CO- at 399.95 eV and -NH2 at 398.80 eV -NH2 was the part of PVAm that did not participate in the cross-linking reaction, while -NH-CO- included the unhydrolyzed part in PVAm and the part formed by cross-linking reaction. Table 3 indicates that the content of -NH-CO- on the surface of the PPPS membrane accounted for 36.39% of the total amount of N element. After cross-linking, the content of -NHCO- on the surface of the T-PPPS membrane rose to 56.82%. At a depth of  10 nm  from  the  T-PPPS  membrane  surface,  it  was  found  that  the composition of N valence was basically the same as that of the PPPS membrane, which indicated that cross-linking reaction did not happen at this depth. Because the detection depth of XPS is usually about 5 nm, it could be inferred that the thickness of the cross-linked layer maybe 5 -10 nm. According to the change of -NH2 content on the surface of the membrane before and after cross-linking, it could also be inferred that about 30% of the surface amine groups participated in the cross-linking reaction.

texts.orig: H2 system. Because of the different compositions of CO2 in the three feed gas, the CO2 partial pressure is different at the same feed pressure. Due to  the  carrier  approaching saturation, when the CO2  partial pressure rose, the CO2  permeance would decrease, which resulted in the difference of the CO2  permeance in the three feed gas under the same feed pressure. Compared with Fig. 11 (b) and Fig. 7 (b), it was found that the selectivity  of  CO2/N2 was  the  highest,  and  that  of  CO2/H2 was  the lowest.  This  was  because  CH4 is  of  stronger  condensation  ability, showing stronger competitive adsorption ability with CO2  than N2. At the same time, the dynamic diameter of the H2 molecule is smaller than that  of  the  CO2  molecule. At 1.0 MPa, for CO2/CH4  system, the CO2 permeance was 391 GPU, and the CO2/CH4 selectivity was 34; for CO2/ H2 system, the CO2 permeance was 243 GPU, and the CO2/H2 selectivity was 10, which indicated that the membrane was also of good CO2 separation performance for natural gas. Fig. 12 showed the comparison of the CO2 separation performance in this work with the upper bounds and the literature data.

texts.orig: 3.5. Preliminary evaluation of the stability for the T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane
texts.text: 3.5. Preliminary evaluation of the stability for the T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As  we  know,  impurity  gases  often  exist  in  the  actual  separation system. CO2/N2 (15/85 by volume) and the simulated flue gas were both used  to  preliminarily  evaluate  the  stability  of  the  T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane (0.1 wt% TMC) and its tolerance to impurity gases with the results being shown in Fig. 13.
texts.text: As  we  know,  impurity  gases  often  exist  in  the  actual  separation system. CO2/N2 (15/85 by volume) and the simulated flue gas were both used  to  preliminarily  evaluate  the  stability  of  the  T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane (0.1 wt% TMC) and its tolerance to impurity gases with the results being shown in Fig. 13.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When switching to the simulated flue gas test, the CO2  permeance and the CO2/N2 selectivity of the membrane both decreased. At 0.2 MPa, the permeance of CO2 decreased to about 850 GPU, and the selectivity of CO2/N2  decreased  to  about  80.  This  was  because  SO2 and  NO2  in simulated flue gas were easier to react with amine groups than CO2. It could also be found that the performance of the membrane could be restored after the feed gas switching back to CO2/N2 (15/85 by volume), indicating that the reaction of amine groups with SO2 and NO2 was also reversible  [68].  The  test  results  showed  that  the  T-PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane (0.1 wt% TMC) was of certain stability and potential in CO2 capture from flue gas. However, 200 h is too short a period to deduce reliable conclusions on the stability of membranes. More experiments of longer time in real separation conditions are needed to evaluate the long-term stability of the prepared membranes.

texts.orig: In this work, the synergistic effect of cationic polymer PVAm with anionic polymer PAAS and small molecular anionic surfactant SDS was utilized to achieve good compatibility between aqueous coating solution in low concentration and hydrophobic surface of PDMS intermediate layer, at the same time increasing the viscosity of the coating solution. Then, by adding PVA of high hydrolysis degree, the PPPS/PDMS/PSf membrane with high gas permeance and moderate CO2 selectivity was prepared, since the water uptake capacity and swelling degree of the separation layer was enhanced. Finally, a 5 -10 nm thick cross-linked layer  was formed on the PPPS surface by simple TMC surface crosslinking,  which  greatly  improved  the  diffusivity  selectivity  and  thus enhanced the CO2 selectivity of the membrane.
